one_line_takeaway: Backdoor defenses that only drive down the ASR of the known trigger leave a broader feature-space 'backdoor region' intact, which a feature-guided PGD attack (FGA) can re-exploit via perceptually different alternative triggers, achieving 63-97% ASR on models 'cleaned' by BAN, NAD, or trigger-aware unlearning.
key_finding: On backdoored ResNet-18/CIFAR-10, WaNet: untargeted PGD selects the target class only 1.03% of the time, while FGA reaches 100% ASR, illustrating that pixel-space-agnostic attacks miss the backdoor region that FGA specifically targets. Table 2 (10% poison rate) reports FGA success rates such as 99.78% (BadNets, ResNet-18/CIFAR-10), 99.53% (Blend), 60.29% (WaNet), and 92.36% (Input-Aware), broadly tracking but sometimes trailing the original ASR. After defenses reduce the ORIGINAL trigger's ASR to near-random -- BAN: 8.58-21.13% across BadNets/WaNet/Blend/Input-Aware on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18; NAD: 7.86-10.43% on the same settings -- FGA-generated alternative triggers still achieve 74.22-87.48% ASR post-BAN and 63.38-85.02% ASR post-NAD. Under trigger-aware unlearning (fine-tuning on the exact recovered trigger), ASR after unlearning remains above 95% for BadNets, WaNet, and Blend on CIFAR-10 on both architectures when re-attacked with newly generated FGA triggers, i.e., unlearning on one trigger instance does not close off the broader feature-space vulnerability; Input-Aware is a partial exception where unlearning drives ASR near-random on CIFAR-10 but not CIFAR-100. Clean-model sanity check: applying the trigger pattern to an unbackdoored model yields ASR near random guessing (~10% on CIFAR-10, ~1% on CIFAR-100, ~0.5% on TinyImageNet), confirming the backdoor region does not pre-exist without poisoning.
core_contribution: The paper argues that current backdoor defenses are 'trigger-centric': they judge a model safe once the attack success rate (ASR) of the known/original trigger has been driven to random chance. The authors show this is insufficient because backdoor training creates a persistent 'backdoor region' in feature space that can be reached by many perceptually distinct 'alternative triggers,' not just the original one. They (1) formalize backdoors as a many-to-one mapping from pixel-space patterns into a shared feature-space region Rt tied to the target label, (2) estimate a 'backdoor direction' d_ell by contrasting mean feature activations of clean vs. originally-triggered inputs at a chosen layer, and (3) introduce Feature-Guided Attack (FGA), a PGD-style optimization that jointly maximizes cross-entropy toward the target label and inner-product alignment with d_ell, to systematically construct alternative triggers. They validate d_ell with a feature-space interpolation experiment and show FGA-crafted alternative triggers remain highly effective (often >60-90% ASR) even after post-training defenses (BAN, NAD, trigger-aware unlearning) have suppressed the original trigger's ASR to near-random levels.
threat_model: Standard backdoor/data-poisoning threat model (attributed to prior work [17,1], not novel to this paper): the attacker controls the training dataset and/or training process. Two scenarios are considered: (1) a poisoned dataset is published on a public repository (e.g., GitHub/HuggingFace) and a victim trains on it unknowingly; (2) a victim outsources training to an untrusted third party who implants the backdoor and returns a compromised model. The attacker selects a target label y_t, applies a trigger transformation to a controlled fraction of training samples, and relabels them (dirty-label poisoning), with no modification to the optimization procedure, loss, or architecture required. The defender's capability is varied across experiments: from no knowledge of the trigger, to successfully reverse-engineering/recovering the exact original trigger and applying post-training repair (BAN, NAD, or trigger-aware unlearning via fine-tuning on trigger-stamped correctly-labeled data).
limitations: The paper itself notes: alternative triggers as constructed here are sample-specific (optimized per input and model), not universal single perturbations; the authors explicitly leave universal alternative-trigger construction and cross-model transferability as future work. FGA's default perturbation budget (epsilon=8/255, up to 32/255 in sweeps) is not constrained for stealthiness -- the authors state their goal is not to discover perceptually stealthy alternative triggers. Evaluation of defenses is restricted to post-training, model-repair defenses (BAN, NAD, trigger-aware unlearning); input-space filtering defenses were excluded by design/argument rather than empirically tested. FGA requires white-box access to internal features and the original trigger (to compute d_ell from paired clean/triggered activations), which is a stronger assumption than a black-box attacker; the paper positions FGA primarily as an analysis/verification tool for defenders and researchers rather than a deployable black-box attack. Only a single feature layer (avgpool) and target label (class 0) were used throughout; sensitivity to layer choice and target-label choice is not explored.

quantitative_results: Clean (unbackdoored) validation accuracy: 79.04% (ResNet-18) / 88.14% (VGG-19) on CIFAR-10; 52.40%/63.89% on CIFAR-100; 53.96%/61.56% on TinyImageNet
Clean-model ASR from applying the trigger pattern (no backdoor present): ~10% CIFAR-10, ~1% CIFAR-100, ~0.5% TinyImageNet
Untargeted PGD target-class selection on backdoored WaNet ResNet-18/CIFAR-10: 1.03%, vs. FGA: 100%
T-PGD ASR (Table 1, ResNet-18) e.g. at eps=8/255: CIFAR-10 BadNets 98.18%, Blend 96.99%, WaNet 99.07%, Input-Aware 22.34%; TinyImageNet BadNets 34.49%, Blend 91.50%, WaNet 50.56%, Input-Aware 10.70%
Table 2 (10% poison rate) FGA ASR, CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: BadNets 99.78%, Blend 99.53%, WaNet 60.29%, Input-Aware 92.36% (original ASRs: 97.30%, 83.98%, 100.0%, 88.32% respectively)
BAN defense (CIFAR-10/ResNet-18, Table 4): ASR after BAN 8.58-21.13% across BadNets/WaNet/Blend/Input-Aware; FGA post-BAN ASR 74.22-87.48%
NAD defense (CIFAR-10/ResNet-18, Table 5): ASR after NAD 7.86-10.43%; FGA post-NAD ASR 63.38-85.02%
Feature interpolation experiment (CIFAR-10, ResNet-18, BadNets, 10% poison): target-class probability ~7% at alpha=0 (clean), ~90% at alpha=0.6, saturating near 100% at alpha=1.0

Trigger-aware unlearning + FGA re-attack: ASR after unlearning stays above 95% for BadNets/WaNet/Blend on CIFAR-10 (both architectures); Input-Aware drops near-random on CIFAR-10 but stays high on CIFAR-100
